name: PurpleAir real-time PM2.5 sensor network
license: PurpleAir API ToS — free API key required, research-permissive
estimated_series_count: 50+ (selectable per sensor)
estimated_length: P5Y+ (historical archive available via separate endpoint)
contamination_reasoning: Citizen-science sensor network requiring API key registration. Data is near-real-time (~2-min latency). Not a standard benchmark dataset; unlikely to appear in pretraining corpora. Future readings are guaranteed unseen.
gap_filled: healthcare × few-min (deficit: 3). Also provides air-quality-as-health-determinant signal distinct from existing OpenAQ/Open-Meteo sources.
difficulty_note: PM2.5 exhibits diurnal patterns but is heavily driven by exogenous events (wildfire smoke, temperature inversions, traffic rush hours). Sensor-to-sensor noise is significant, and spatial heterogeneity across a city panel creates a challenging hierarchical forecasting problem. Not trivially periodic — a seasonal-naive baseline would fail during smoke events.
adapter_notes: Register at purpleair.com for API key. GET /v1/sensors returns current readings; paginate with max_results. Historical data via /v1/sensors/{id}/stats. Rate limit: 1 request/sec on free tier. Poll every 2-5 min to build time series. JSON response. Select ~50 sensors across diverse geographies for a panel.
verify: Confirm API key registration process and current rate limits. Verify that historical data access is sufficient for backtesting (the stats endpoint returns daily aggregates, not raw 2-min readings — may need to build series by polling forward).

name: Steam Web API — concurrent players per game (real-time polling)
license: Steam Web API ToS — public endpoint, no key required for this method
estimated_series_count: 30+ (one per game/appid)
estimated_length: Polling-based: accumulates from first poll forward; SteamDB historical archives go back ~10 years
contamination_reasoning: Real-time concurrent player counts polled via API. Future values are generated live and cannot have been in any pretraining corpus. The endpoint is public but the resulting time series (per-game concurrent players at 5-min cadence) is not a known benchmark dataset.
gap_filled: sales × few-min (deficit: 3). Also partially addresses sales × half-hour if polled at 30-min intervals for a secondary panel.
difficulty_note: Player counts have strong diurnal (timezone-driven) and weekly (weekend peaks) seasonality, but also event-driven spikes (game updates, Steam sales, esports tournaments) and long-term decay curves as games lose popularity. Different games have radically different profiles (live-service vs. single-player), creating a diverse panel that tests regime adaptation.
adapter_notes: No API key needed for GetNumberOfCurrentPlayers. Poll every 5 min for a panel of ~30 appids (mix of popular and niche games). Response is JSON: {response: {player_count, result}}. Rate limit is generous (~100k calls/day). Store timestamp + count per poll. Select games with diverse player-base sizes for panel diversity.
verify: Confirm endpoint is still keyless (Steam has been tightening API access). Verify that SteamDB or Steam's own historical data can be used for backtesting, or plan to accumulate series forward-only.

name: NIST NVD CVE API — vulnerability disclosure arrivals
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate arrival rate) or multiple per CVE category
contamination_reasoning: Event-driven disclosure arrivals. Future CVE publications are inherently unseen. While the CVE database itself is well-known in cybersecurity, it is not a standard time-series benchmark dataset and the arrival-rate time series is a derived product unlikely to be in any pretraining corpus.
gap_filled: web_cloudops × irregular (deficit: 3). First irregular/event-driven source for this domain.
difficulty_note: CVE disclosure rate has a strong weekly pattern (fewer on weekends), a monthly cycle (Microsoft Patch Tuesday, CISA advisories), and an increasing secular trend. But it also has irregular bursts (zero-day disclosures, large vulnerability campaigns like Log4Shell) that make it non-trivially periodic. Count series with zero-inflation on quiet days and heavy tails on event days.
adapter_notes: Free API, no key needed but rate-limited to 5 requests/30 sec without key (50 with key). Query by date range to get daily/hourly arrival counts. JSON response with CVE records including publishedDate. Aggregate by time window to build count series. For real-time, poll the last 24h window every 15-30 min.
verify: Confirm rate limits and whether the API key (free) is required for practical use. Verify date-range query syntax and response pagination.

name: SEC EDGAR — filing submission arrival rate
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate) or multiple per filing type (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K, S-1, etc.)
contamination_reasoning: Filing arrivals are event-driven and future-generated. While EDGAR is well-known, the filing arrival-rate time series is a derived aggregate not found in standard TS benchmark corpora. The full-text search API provides real-time access to recent filings.
gap_filled: econ_fin × irregular (deficit: 3). First irregular/event-driven source for econ_fin. Adds a non-market-price financial signal.
difficulty_note: Filing rate has strong weekly seasonality (very few filings on weekends), quarterly peaks (10-Q filings in earnings season), and annual cycles (10-K filings 60-90 days after fiscal year end). But there are also irregular bursts (restatements, 8-K material events, IPO waves) that break the periodic pattern. Different filing types have different seasonal structures, enabling a multi-series panel.
adapter_notes: EDGAR full-text search API at efts.sec.gov. No API key needed but rate-limited (~10 req/sec). Query by date range and form type. JSON response includes hit count and filing details. For real-time, poll last 24h every 15-30 min. For historical, query by date range and aggregate counts per day/hour. Respect SEC's fair access policy (max 10 req/sec).
verify: Confirm the efts.sec.gov endpoint is still active and returns JSON. Verify that the search API supports date-range queries for historical backfill. Check whether form-type filtering works as documented.

name: WHO Disease Outbreak News (DON) — event arrival stream
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate event rate) or multiple per disease category
contamination_reasoning: Event-driven outbreak announcements. Future outbreaks are inherently unseen. The DON archive is not a standard TS benchmark dataset. WHO's RSS/HTML scraping approach means the data is not in a clean CSV format that would appear in pretraining corpora.
gap_filled: healthcare × irregular (deficit: 3). First irregular/event-driven source for healthcare. Adds a genuinely different signal type (outbreak event arrivals) to a domain currently dominated by count-based surveillance.
difficulty_note: Outbreak announcement rate has seasonal patterns (flu season, dengue season), multi-year regimes (pandemic vs. inter-pandemic), and irregular bursts (novel pathogen emergence, outbreak clusters). The series is sparse (many days with zero announcements) with heavy-tailed bursts. Requires handling zero-inflation and event clustering.
adapter_notes: WHO publishes DON as HTML pages with an archive. No official API. Scrape the listing page for recent DON entries, or use WHO's RSS feed if available. Poll every 30-60 min for new entries. Parse publication dates to build event arrival time series. Historical archive available via the DON index page with pagination. Respect WHO's robots.txt and rate limits.
verify: Confirm whether WHO provides an RSS feed for DON (check www.who.int/rss-feeds/). If no RSS, verify that the HTML scraping approach is feasible and compliant with WHO ToS. Check historical archive depth and pagination structure.

name: ENTSO-E Transparency Platform — unavailability of generation units
license: ENTSO-E Transparency Platform ToS — free registration required for API key
estimated_series_count: Multiple (per control area / generation unit type)
contamination_reasoning: Grid outage/unavailability events are operational data published in real-time. Future outages are inherently unseen. ENTSO-E data is used in energy research but the unavailability event stream is not a standard TS benchmark dataset and requires API registration.
gap_filled: energy × irregular (deficit: 3). First irregular/event-driven source for energy. Adds outage-event forecasting, which is structurally different from the price/demand series currently dominating the energy domain.
difficulty_note: Generation unit unavailability has weather-driven patterns (cold weather outages, storm damage), seasonal maintenance windows, and irregular event bursts (cascading failures, fuel supply disruptions). The series is zero-inflated (most units are available most of the time) with clustered outage events. Forecasting outage probability and duration is a classification+regression hybrid problem.
adapter_notes: Register at transparency.entsoe.eu for free API key (securityToken). REST API returns XML. Query by document type, domain (control area), and date range. For unavailability data, use the appropriate document type code (verify in ENTSO-E documentation — likely under 'Unavailability of Generation' category). Paginate by date range. Rate limit: be conservative, ~1 req/2sec.
verify: Confirm the exact document type code for generation unit unavailability (check ENTSO-E Transparency Platform documentation at https://transparency.entsoe.eu/content/static_content/Static%20content/web%20api/Guide.html). Verify API registration process and that unavailability data is published in real-time. Confirm XML response format and parsing requirements.

name: EMSC Seismic Portal — European-Mediterranean real-time earthquake events
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate event stream) or multiple per magnitude bin
contamination_reasoning: Real-time earthquake event stream from the European-Mediterranean Seismological Centre. Distinct from USGS (already in pool) — different network, different region coverage, independent processing. Event arrivals are inherently future-generated. Not a standard TS benchmark dataset.
gap_filled: nature × irregular (deficit: 3). First irregular/event-driven source for nature domain. Complements existing USGS/INGV seismic sources with independent European-Mediterranean coverage.
difficulty_note: Earthquake arrivals are approximately Poisson but exhibit clustering (aftershock sequences, swarm events), magnitude-dependent frequency (Gutenberg-Richter), and spatial heterogeneity. Forecasting event counts in time bins requires handling overdispersion and temporal clustering — a fundamentally different challenge from smooth periodic series.
adapter_notes: FDSNWS event API, standard format. Query with minmagnitude, starttime, endtime parameters. JSON or text format. No API key needed. For real-time, poll every 5-10 min for new events. Historical data available via same endpoint with date ranges. Rate limit: be respectful, ~1 req/10sec.
verify: Confirm endpoint is operational and returns JSON. Verify that the EMSC portal provides sufficient historical depth for backtesting (check earliest available date via query).

name: PJM Data Miner 2 — Real-time LMP & Load (5-min)
license: PJM public data — API key required (free registration via pjm.com)
estimated_series_count: 20+ (zonal + nodes)
contamination_reasoning: PJM is a well-known ISO but its Data Miner API is not a standard TS benchmark dataset. Real-time 5-min LMPs are highly volatile, non-periodic, and continuously generated — future values cannot have been pretrained on. The API requires registration and is not commonly scraped into ML corpora.
gap_filled: energy × sub-min (deficit 3); also strengthens energy × few-min
difficulty_note: Real-time LMPs exhibit extreme volatility with price spikes, negative prices, and regime shifts driven by weather, outages, and fuel costs. A seasonal-naive baseline fails catastrophically. Strong discriminator between models that can handle heavy-tailed, non-stationary series.
adapter_notes: Register at pjm.com for an API subscription key. REST API with JSON responses. Pagination via record_count and start_record. Rate limits apply. Multiple endpoints available: real-time LMP, load, ancillary services. Poll every 5 min for live data; historical bulk download available.
verify: Confirm the exact endpoint path and parameter names against the PJM Data Miner 2 API documentation at https://www.pjm.com/markets-and-operations/etools/data-miner-2. Verify that the free registration tier permits the needed query volume.

name: openFDA — Food & Drug Recall Enforcement Reports
license: Public domain (US Government / openFDA) — no API key required, rate-limited
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate count) or 10+ (by classification/recalling firm)
contamination_reasoning: FDA recall enforcement reports as a time series are not part of any known TS benchmark or pretraining corpus. The openFDA API is public but the data is not commonly used for forecasting. New recalls are published continuously, so future data is contamination-free.
difficulty_note: Recall events cluster around food safety outbreaks and regulatory actions, creating bursty, zero-inflated count series with non-stationary trends (regulatory changes, supply chain disruptions). Seasonality exists (agricultural cycles, holiday production) but is weak. Event clustering and heavy tails make this a strong discriminator.
adapter_notes: No API key needed for low-volume use (240 requests/min, 120000/day). JSON responses. Supports date range search via report_date field. Also available: drug enforcement (https://api.fda.gov/drug/enforcement.json) and device enforcement. Aggregate by day or week for count time series. Can also track by classification (Class I/II/III) for hierarchical structure.
verify: Confirm the report_date field name and date format (YYYYMMDD) in the search syntax. Verify that food, drug, and device enforcement endpoints are still active and return historical data back to ~2004.

name: SEC EDGAR — Current Filings Feed (Atom/RSS)
license: Public domain (US Government / SEC) — no key required, rate-limited (10 req/sec)
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate filing rate) or 10+ (by form type: 10-K, 10-Q, 8-K, S-1, etc.)
contamination_reasoning: SEC EDGAR filings are public and well-known, but the filing-rate time series (counts of new filings per time window) is not part of any known TS benchmark. The data is continuously generated, and future filing events are contamination-proof. While EDGAR itself is well-known, using it as an event-count time series is novel.
difficulty_note: Filing rates have strong weekly seasonality (weekday vs. weekend), quarterly cycles (earnings/10-Q deadlines), and annual cycles (10-K season). But they also have event-driven spikes (IPO waves, regulatory deadlines, market crises) and structural breaks (SEC rule changes, electronic filing adoption). Multi-seasonal with regime shifts — strong discriminator.
adapter_notes: No API key required. SEC requests User-Agent header set. Atom XML format. Poll every few minutes for live data; use start/count for pagination. Can filter by form type via type= parameter (e.g., type=10-K). Aggregate into per-hour or per-day filing counts. Historical data available via EDGAR full-text search at https://efts.sec.gov/LATEST/search-index.
verify: Confirm the Atom feed endpoint is still active and returns recent filings. Verify the User-Agent header requirement. Check if historical filing timestamps are available for backfilling (may need the EDGAR index files at https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/daily-index/).

name: PurpleAir Community API — PM2.5 & meteorological sensor network
license: PurpleAir Data API — free API key required for research (request via purpleair.com/contact)
estimated_series_count: 30+ (select sensors across US/Global)
estimated_length: P8Y+ (historical via API; live via polling)
contamination_reasoning: PurpleAir is a citizen-science sensor network with 30,000+ sensors globally. It is not part of any known TS benchmark. The API requires a key and is not commonly used in ML research. Data is continuously generated, so future values are contamination-proof. Distinct from OpenAQ (which aggregates official monitors) and sensor.community (European-focused).
gap_filled: nature × few-min (deficit 0→filled); also fills healthcare × few-min as air-quality proxy; contributes to nature × half-hour via aggregation
difficulty_note: PM2.5 has diurnal patterns (rush hour, inversion) but is dominated by episodic wildfire smoke events causing massive regime shifts. Sensor noise, calibration drift, and hyperlocal effects add difficulty. The wildfire-smoke regime shifts are exactly the kind of non-stationarity that separates strong models from seasonal-naive baselines.
adapter_notes: Request an API key via purpleair.com/contact (free for research). REST API with JSON. The v1 API requires the key as a query parameter. Sensors endpoint returns current readings; use the history endpoint (https://api.purpleair.com/v1/sensors/{sensor_index}/history) for historical data with configurable averaging periods. Select 30+ sensors across diverse geographies (urban, rural, wildfire-prone). Rate limit: 60 req/min.
verify: Confirm the v1 API is still active and the fields parameter names are correct. Verify the history endpoint exists and supports 2-min averaging. Check if the API key request process is still free for research use. Confirm sensor coverage in target regions.

name: USGS Volcano Notifications Service — Eruption & Unrest Alerts
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate alert count) or 5+ (per observatory: AVO, CVO, HVO, YVO, LVO)
contamination_reasoning: Volcanic alert data is extremely niche — not part of any TS benchmark or pretraining corpus. The USGS Volcano Notifications Service (VNS) publishes alerts as events occur, so future data is contamination-proof. The data is obscure even within geoscience ML.
difficulty_note: Volcanic alerts are extremely sparse (zero-inflated), with long quiescent periods punctuated by eruption sequences. Clustering during volcanic crises, seasonal variations in unrest detection (weather affecting monitoring), and non-stationary monitoring capabilities make this a hard event-driven forecasting problem. A naive baseline would predict zero always — any model that can forecast eruption sequences demonstrates real skill.
adapter_notes: The USGS VHP updates page is HTML — requires scraping. Check for an RSS/Atom feed (historically available at volcanoes.usgs.gov). Alternatively, the USGS VNS email notification system can be subscribed to. Historical weekly reports available at https://volcanoes.usgs.gov/vhp/weekly_reports.html. Aggregate alert counts by day or week per observatory. May also check the Smithsonian GVP weekly volcanic activity reports at https://volcano.si.edu/reports_weekly.cfm for a complementary source.
verify: Confirm whether an RSS/Atom feed exists for USGS volcano alerts (check https://volcanoes.usgs.gov/vns/ and the VHP updates page). If no feed, verify the HTML scraping approach and the availability of historical weekly reports. Also check the Smithsonian GVP weekly report RSS feed as an alternative or complementary source.

name: Global Fishing Watch API — Vessel Encounter & Port Visit Events
license: Global Fishing Watch API — free API key required (registration at globalfishingwatch.org/our-api)
estimated_series_count: 5+ (by region: Pacific, Atlantic, Indian Ocean, etc.)
contamination_reasoning: GFW vessel encounter events (two vessels in close proximity at sea) and port visit events are highly niche maritime data. Not part of any TS benchmark or pretraining corpus. The API requires registration and is used primarily in ocean conservation research. Future events are contamination-proof.
difficulty_note: Vessel encounters have seasonal patterns (fishing seasons, migratory routes) but are driven by complex economic, regulatory, and environmental factors. Encounter events cluster spatially and temporally (fishing grounds, transshipment hotspots). Zero-inflated with non-stationary trends (regulatory changes like IUU fishing crackdowns, seasonal closures). Port visits have tidal/day-of-week patterns but also economic cycle effects. Strong discriminator for spatio-temporal event models.
adapter_notes: Register at globalfishingwatch.org/our-api for a free API key. REST API with JSON. Datasets include: encounters (vessel-to-vessel), port_visits, fishing_effort. Supports spatial and temporal filtering. Rate limits: moderate (check documentation). Aggregate events into daily/weekly counts by region for time series. Historical data available from ~2016.
verify: Confirm the v2 API is active and the events endpoint supports the encounters and port_visits datasets. Verify the registration process for an API key. Check the spatial coverage (global vs. select regions) and the temporal depth of historical data. Confirm rate limits.

name: NASA FIRMS active fire detections (MODIS + VIIRS)
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregated event stream; can be partitioned by region)
contamination_reasoning: Fire detection event streams are not a standard TSFM pretraining format. The data is satellite-pass-driven and spatially referenced, not a clean univariate series. Post-cutoff data is continuously generated. The API requires a free MAP_KEY registration, adding a small access friction that reduces scraping likelihood.
gap_filled: nature × irregular — first irregular/event-driven nature source
difficulty_note: Point-process data with strong diurnal (satellite orbital pass) and seasonal (dry season) clustering, spatial correlation, and burst behavior during large wildfire events. A seasonal-naive baseline would fail because events are sparse and bursty, not smoothly periodic.
adapter_notes: Register for a free MAP_KEY at https://firms.modaps.eosdis.nasa.gov/api/mapkey/. Poll the area endpoint for a bounding box; returns CSV with lat/lon/brightness/acquisition_time/confidence. Rate limit: ~50 requests/hour on free tier. Can also use the bulk download via https://firms.modaps.eosdis.nasa.gov/active_fire/ for historical backfill.
verify: Confirm MAP_KEY registration flow still works and that the CSV response includes scan_time (not just acquisition_date). Check whether VIIRS 375m data is available via the same API or a separate endpoint.

name: RIPE RIS Live — real-time BGP update event stream
estimated_series_count: 1 (BGP update event rate; can be partitioned by peer AS)
estimated_length: P20Y (RIS collecting since 1999; live stream available since ~2019)
contamination_reasoning: BGP update event streams are not a known TSFM pretraining format. The existing RIPE source in the pool (ripestat_announced_prefixes) is a snapshot API, not an event stream. The WebSocket stream is real-time and future data cannot have been pretrained on. BGP event data is niche and primarily consumed by network researchers, not ML practitioners.
gap_filled: web_cloudops × irregular — first irregular/event-driven web_cloudops source
difficulty_note: Highly bursty point process: BGP updates arrive in cascades during routing incidents, prefix hijacks, or fiber cuts. Most of the time the rate is low and stable; during events it spikes by orders of magnitude. The inter-arrival distribution is heavy-tailed. Models must capture both the baseline rate and the burst structure.
adapter_notes: Connect to the WebSocket endpoint. Subscribe to BGP updates with optional filters (e.g., peer AS, prefix). Each message is a JSON object with timestamp, peer, prefix, and update type (announce/withdraw). Aggregate into per-second or per-minute counts. For historical backfill, RIPE RIS collects raw BGP data available via FTP at https://www.ripe.net/analyse/internet-measurements/routing-information-service-ris. No authentication required for the live stream.
verify: Confirm the WebSocket endpoint is active and returns BGP update messages. Check whether historical RIS data is accessible via bulk download for backfill. Verify message format and fields.

name: Twitch Helix API — live stream viewer counts (top 100 streams aggregate)
license: Twitch Developer Agreement — requires OAuth token (Client ID + Client Secret)
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate viewer count across top 100 streams; can be partitioned by game/category)
estimated_length: P10Y (Twitch API since ~2015; Helix API since 2018)
contamination_reasoning: Twitch viewer count time series are not a known TSFM pretraining format. The data requires OAuth authentication and API registration, adding access friction. Historical data is not available as a bulk download — only the current state can be queried, so any time series must be accumulated going forward.
gap_filled: sales × few-min — second sub-hourly sales source, different platform from Steam
difficulty_note: Strong diurnal patterns (peak evening US/EU time), weekly seasonality, but also event-driven spikes: esports tournaments, game releases, 'raid' mechanics, and streamer-specific events. The aggregate is non-stationary as the platform grows. Category-level partitioning reveals different temporal signatures (e.g., Just Chatting vs. competitive games).
adapter_notes: Register a Twitch application at https://dev.twitch.tv/console to get Client ID and Client Secret. Use the OAuth client-credentials flow to get an app access token. Call /helix/streams?first=100 to get current live streams with viewer_count. Poll at 1–5 min intervals. Rate limit: 800 points/min (each request costs 1 point). Token expires ~60 days; implement refresh logic.
verify: Confirm the OAuth client-credentials flow works for /helix/streams (some endpoints require user OAuth). Verify rate limit calculation. Check whether Twitch provides any historical viewer count data (unlikely, but confirm).

name: DOE OE-417 electric disturbance event log
estimated_series_count: 1 (disturbance event arrival rate; can be partitioned by NERC region/event type)
contamination_reasoning: Electric disturbance event logs are not a known TSFM pretraining format. The data is hosted on a DOE/NETL portal that is not commonly scraped by ML researchers. Events are reported with a delay (hours to days), but the event timestamps themselves are accurate. The data is niche and primarily consumed by reliability engineers.
gap_filled: energy × irregular — first irregular/event-driven energy source
difficulty_note: Weather-correlated event process: hurricanes, heat waves, and winter storms create clusters of disturbance events. Seasonal patterns (summer thunderstorms, winter ice storms). Cascading failure patterns where one event triggers subsequent ones. The event types (voltage dip, frequency excursion, islanding) have different temporal signatures.
adapter_notes: The OE-417 page publishes incident reports as they are filed. Scrape the listing for event date/time, NERC region, event type, and affected MW. Historical data is available in annual summaries. The page may require HTML parsing (not a clean API). Rate limit: be respectful, ~1 req/min. Note: there is typically a reporting delay of 1–7 days between event occurrence and publication.
verify: Confirm the OE-417 page is still actively updated and accessible. Check whether historical annual summaries are available for backfill. Verify the data format (HTML table vs. PDF vs. CSV). Investigate whether NERC GADS (Generating Availability Data System) has a more accessible public subset.

name: npm registry CouchDB _changes feed — package publish event stream
license: npm public data — research use permitted
estimated_series_count: 1 (package publish event rate; can be partitioned by scope/maintainer)
contamination_reasoning: Package publish event streams are not a known TSFM pretraining format. The existing npm sources in the pool track daily download counts — this is a fundamentally different data product (event arrivals vs. aggregated counts). The CouchDB _changes feed is a replication protocol endpoint that is not commonly consumed by ML practitioners. Future publish events cannot have been pretrained on.
gap_filled: sales × irregular — first irregular/event-driven sales source; complements existing daily npm download counts
difficulty_note: Diurnal and weekly patterns (developers publish more during US/EU business hours), but also event-driven spikes: security advisories trigger mass republishes, new framework releases cause dependency cascades, and coordinated multi-package updates create bursts. The process is non-stationary (npm's growth has changed the baseline rate over time). Long-tailed inter-arrival distribution.
adapter_notes: The _changes feed is a CouchDB long-poll or continuous stream. Use feed=continuous for a streaming connection, or feed=longpoll for a single batch. Each change includes a sequence number, document ID (package name), and revision. Filter to only include package publishes (not metadata updates) by checking the document's versions field. For historical backfill, use since=0 to get all changes from the beginning (very large). Rate limit: the replicate endpoint is designed for replication, so it's fairly tolerant, but be reasonable.
verify: Confirm the replicate.npmjs.com endpoint is accessible and returns changes. Verify the feed=continuous parameter works for streaming. Check whether the full historical changes feed (since=0) is practically accessible for backfill or too large. Confirm that document changes include enough metadata to distinguish publishes from other updates.

name: OpenSky Network flights API — arrival/departure events per airport
estimated_series_count: 1 per airport (suggest 5-airport panel: JFK, LHR, HND, DXB, ATL)
contamination_reasoning: Flight arrival/departure event streams are not a standard TSFM dataset. The existing OpenSky source in the pool (states/all) tracks real-time aircraft positions, which is a different data product. Flight event logs are derived and less commonly scraped. Post-cutoff data is continuously generated.
gap_filled: transport × irregular — first irregular/event-driven transport source
difficulty_note: Schedule-driven periodicity (airline schedules create strong diurnal/weekly patterns) but with significant noise from delays, cancellations, weather disruptions, and seasonal capacity changes. The inter-arrival distribution is non-stationary (changes by time-of-day). Models must distinguish scheduled from actual arrival times.
adapter_notes: Query the flights endpoint with begin/end timestamps (Unix epoch). Returns JSON array of flight objects with firstSeen/lastSeen/estDepartureAirport/estArrivalAirport. Rate limit: 400 requests/day for anonymous, 4000/day for registered users. The endpoint has a max time range of 2 hours per query. Already have OpenSky states/all in pool — this is a different endpoint returning discrete flight events, not continuous states.
verify: Confirm the flights/arrival endpoint is distinct from the states/all endpoint already in the pool. Check whether historical flight data is accessible or only recent (30-day window). Verify rate limits for registered vs. anonymous access.

name: Fingrid Open Data API — Grid Frequency (1-min)
contamination_reasoning: High-frequency grid frequency data is rarely included in TSFM pretraining corpora, which typically focus on daily or hourly macro series. Live future data is guaranteed uncontaminated.
difficulty_note: Grid frequency is a chaotic, mean-reverting series with sudden regime shifts caused by generation outages or load drops. It requires modeling both high-frequency noise and low-frequency stability.
adapter_notes: Use the Fingrid open data API. Dataset ID 177 provides 1-minute frequency measurements. No auth required for basic queries, but rate limits apply. Format is JSON.
verify: Verify the exact dataset ID for 1-min frequency and the historical lookback window available via the API.

name: WAQI (World Air Quality Index) API — Real-time Station Measurements
contamination_reasoning: Hyper-local air quality station data at 1-minute resolution is highly unlikely to be in pretraining sets. The WAQI API provides live data that postdates all model cutoffs.
difficulty_note: Air quality is influenced by complex local factors (traffic, weather, industrial activity) leading to noisy, non-stationary regimes with sudden spikes that are hard to predict.
adapter_notes: Register for a free API token at waqi.info. Query specific station IDs. The API returns real-time and recent historical data (usually up to 2 years). Rate limits depend on token tier.
verify: Check the maximum historical lookback available for a given station via the /history endpoint.

name: NASA EONET — Earth Observatory Natural Event Tracker
contamination_reasoning: A curated feed of global natural events (wildfires, storms, volcanoes). Unlikely to be in pretraining as a time series, and live events are inherently post-cutoff.
difficulty_note: Event arrival times are stochastic and clustered. Forecasting requires modeling point processes rather than continuous values.
adapter_notes: REST API returns JSON. No auth required. Can filter by date, category, and source. Build a time series by binning event counts into discrete intervals.
verify: Check the API documentation for the exact date range of available historical events.

name: CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog
contamination_reasoning: A government feed of actively exploited vulnerabilities. Updated irregularly as new threats emerge. Not a standard time-series dataset.
difficulty_note: Arrival of new CVEs is a pure point process driven by external threat actors. No seasonal pattern, making it a strong test of a model's ability to handle pure stochasticity.
adapter_notes: Download the JSON feed periodically. Track the 'dateAdded' field to construct an irregular event time series or a daily count series.
verify: Ensure the JSON schema is stable and track the 'dateAdded' field for time series construction.

name: Cloudflare Radar API — Internet Traffic & Outages
contamination_reasoning: Aggregated global internet traffic and outage data. Proprietary to Cloudflare, ensuring it has not been scraped into open pretraining datasets.
difficulty_note: Internet traffic exhibits strong diurnal patterns but is punctuated by sudden, sharp outages. Models must handle both the smooth periodic baseline and the heavy-tailed anomaly spikes.
adapter_notes: Requires a Cloudflare account and API token with Radar read permissions. Returns JSON. Available in 15-min or 30-min aggregations.
verify: Verify the specific endpoints available for time-series traffic vs. outage annotations, and the historical lookback limit.

name: NOAA SPC Storm Reports (real-time + archive)
license: Public domain (NOAA / NWS Storm Prediction Center)
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate event count) or 4 (by type: tornado, hail, wind, flood)
contamination_reasoning: Storm reports are raw event logs from NWS forecast offices, not a packaged time-series dataset. The real-time CSV is regenerated daily. Historical archives are large CSV files on spc.noaa.gov, not distributed through standard ML data channels. Unlikely to be in any TSFM pretraining corpus.
difficulty_note: Severe weather events are spatially clustered, seasonally dependent (spring peak for tornadoes, summer for wind) but highly variable year-to-year. Strong regime-switching behavior (active vs. quiet patterns). Zero-inflated at daily resolution. Count data with overdispersion — Poisson baseline would fail.
adapter_notes: Real-time: fetch today.csv (updated throughout the day). Archive: bulk CSV downloads from /wcm/data/. No auth, no rate limit documented but be respectful. Fields include timestamp, state, latitude/longitude, magnitude, type. Parse timestamps carefully — mixed formats across years.
verify: Confirm today.csv endpoint is still active and has today's date in the data. Check archive CSV column consistency across decades (schema has changed).

name: SEC EDGAR — Filing Submission Events (per-filer aggregate)
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate filing rate) or 6+ (by form type: 10-K, 10-Q, 8-K, S-1, 13F, 4)
contamination_reasoning: SEC EDGAR is a regulatory filing system, not a time-series dataset. While the raw filings are public, the filing-event arrival process (timestamps of submissions) is not packaged as a forecasting benchmark anywhere. The full-text search API is relatively new and not commonly scraped for ML. Filing events are operational timestamps, not market prices.
difficulty_note: Filing arrivals have strong quarterly clustering (10-K/10-Q deadlines), weekly patterns (Friday filings), and intra-day clustering (just before deadlines). But within these patterns, arrival times are bursty and event-driven. 8-K filings (material events) are genuinely unpredictable. Hierarchical by form type and industry. Count data with overdispersion.
adapter_notes: Per-filer: fetch submissions JSON, extract filingDate array. Aggregate across filers for event-count series. Search API: use efts.sec.gov with form type and date filters. Rate limit: 10 req/sec, must set User-Agent header with email. No auth required. Paginate via offset.
verify: Confirm data.sec.gov/submissions/CIK{cik}.json returns filing dates. Check that efts.sec.gov search API supports date-range queries. Verify User-Agent requirement is still enforced.

name: PhysioNet MIMIC-IV Waveform Database — bedside vital signs
license: PhysioNet Credentialed Health Data License (requires CITI training + data use agreement)
estimated_series_count: 100+ (one per patient stay; select a panel of 30-50 with >48h continuous recording)
contamination_reasoning: MIMIC-IV requires credentialed access (CITI human-subjects training + signed DUA). It cannot be bulk-downloaded without credentials and has never been included in public ML benchmark archives. While MIMIC-IV clinical data is known, the waveform database (high-frequency vital signs) is a separate, less-accessed resource. No TSFM pretraining corpus would include credentialed clinical waveforms.
gap_filled: healthcare × sub-min (deficit 3); also contributes to healthcare × few-min
difficulty_note: Bedside vital signs (heart rate, SpO2, blood pressure, respiratory rate) are multi-scale: fast oscillations within minutes, trend changes over hours, regime shifts at clinical events (medication administration, procedures). Non-stationary within each patient. Strong autocorrelation but with abrupt step changes. Artifact-prone. Fundamentally different from any existing source in the pool.
adapter_notes: Access requires PhysioNet credentialing (free but takes 1-2 weeks). Data is in WFDB format (.hea/.dat files). Use the wfdb Python library to read. Sample at 1-min resolution for sub-min cadence, or use native 1-sec for true sub-minute. Select patients with >48h continuous waveform coverage. Multiple vital sign channels per patient = multivariate series.
verify: Confirm MIMIC-IV-WDB version and patient count. Check that vital sign channels (HR, SpO2, ABP) are available for selected stays. Verify the credentialing process is still as described.
_vet_reasons: claims low contamination but is not live and first_available_date '2008-07-01 (data collection period 2008-2019)' is not clearly after model cutoff 2024-03-01 — justify or downgrade

name: NYC EMS Incident Dispatch Data
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregated to counts per time bin) or 5+ (by borough / severity)
contamination_reasoning: NYC Open Data publishes this via Socrata, but it is an operational dispatch log, not a curated ML benchmark dataset. It has never appeared in GluonTS/Darts/Monash archives. The data is continuously appended with new incidents, so future rows postdate all model cutoffs.
gap_filled: healthcare × irregular (deficit 3); also contributes to healthcare few-min/half-hour if aggregated
difficulty_note: Count data with temporal clustering around weather events, mass gatherings, and seasonal respiratory illness waves. Zero-inflated at quiet hours, bursty during incidents. Inter-arrival times are non-exponential. A seasonal-naive baseline would miss event-driven spikes entirely.
adapter_notes: Socrata API (SoQL). No auth needed for basic queries; rate limit ~1000 req/hr anonymous. Paginate with $limit and $offset. Key fields: incident_dispatch_time, incident_datetime, borough, severity_level. Aggregate to counts per 15-min or 1-hour bin for regular-cadence tasks, or use raw inter-arrival times for irregular tasks.
verify: Confirm the dataset ID 76xm-jjuj is still active on data.cityofnewyork.us. Check that incident_dispatch_time is populated for recent records (some fields have gaps in early years).

name: Pecan Street Dataport — residential electricity consumption & solar generation
url_or_endpoint: https://dataport.pecanstreet.org/ (web portal + SQL access); data API at https://dataport.cloud/api/
license: Pecan Street research agreement (academic access free with institutional sponsorship)
estimated_series_count: 50-200 (one per household; select a panel across climates)
estimated_length: P5Y-P12Y per household (varies by enrollment date)
contamination_reasoning: Pecan Street is a research consortium with controlled access. Data is not publicly downloadable and has never appeared in any ML benchmark archive. Individual household circuits are obscure and location-specific. Requires institutional agreement, making bulk scraping into pretraining corpora impractical.
difficulty_note: Residential load at 1-minute resolution has multi-seasonal structure (daily occupancy patterns, weekly work schedules) but is dominated by appliance on/off events — step changes when AC, oven, or EV charger activates. Solar generation adds weather-driven volatility. Non-stationary across seasons. Hierarchical (circuit → household → neighborhood). Fundamentally different from grid-level aggregate data already in the pool.
adapter_notes: Academic access via institutional sponsorship. Data accessed via SQL queries to Pecan Street's database or via their REST API. 1-minute resolution for most households; 1-second available for some. Key fields: use_kw, gen_kw, solar_kw per circuit. Select households with >3 years of continuous data. Rate limits depend on access tier.
verify: Confirm the API endpoint and access model (SQL vs REST). Check whether 1-minute data is available for a sufficient number of households. Verify the academic access process and whether new enrollments are accepted.

name: CAIDA IODA (Internet Outage Detection and Analysis)
estimated_series_count: 1 (global outage count/severity)
contamination_reasoning: IODA is a research tool from CAIDA. Its API is not a standard commercial or benchmark dataset and is unlikely to be memorized.
gap_filled: web_cloudops x half-hour, web_cloudops x irregular
difficulty_note: Signals are zero-inflated with sudden, massive spikes during geopolitical events or major infrastructure failures. Regime-switching behavior is extreme.
adapter_notes: REST API returns JSON. Can filter by geographic region or ASN. No auth required for basic queries.
verify: Check the exact granularity of the 'from' and 'until' parameters (typically 15-min or 1-hour bins).

name: ERCOT (Electric Reliability Council of Texas) Real-Time Outlook
estimated_series_count: 1 (system-wide demand + supply)
contamination_reasoning: Live operational dashboard API. While ERCOT data is public, this specific JSON endpoint is designed for real-time dashboards, not historical bulk download, making it unlikely to be in TSFM corpora.
difficulty_note: Highly volatile, weather-sensitive load with extreme regime shifts during Texas weather events (e.g., winter storms). Non-stationary and chaotic.
adapter_notes: Returns JSON with current and forecasted demand. Poll every 5 minutes. No auth required.
verify: Verify the historical availability of this specific endpoint; bulk historical data may require the MIS or data miner.

name: Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM) API
estimated_series_count: 10 (route panel)
contamination_reasoning: Regional transit API, not a global standard dataset.
gap_filled: transport x few-min, transport x half-hour
difficulty_note: Transit data with schedule-based periodicity disrupted by traffic and incidents. Good for testing deviation from expected schedules.
adapter_notes: Requires an API key. Returns JSON. Poll every 1-5 minutes for bus/tram locations or arrivals.
verify: Confirm the API is still active and accepting new research registrations.

name: OpenAPS Data Commons
estimated_series_count: 20 (patient panel)
contamination_reasoning: Niche, patient-generated health data. Unlikely to be in general pretraining corpora.
gap_filled: healthcare x few-min, healthcare x half-hour
difficulty_note: Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM) data. Noisy, non-stationary, with regime shifts based on meals and insulin. Highly personal and difficult to forecast.
adapter_notes: Bulk download from the OpenAPS Data Commons S3 bucket or website. Data is in JSON/CSV. 5-minute cadence.
verify: Verify the data format and the process for requesting access to the data commons.
_vet_reasons: claims low contamination but is not live and first_available_date '2018-01-01' is not clearly after model cutoff 2024-03-01 — justify or downgrade

name: UK National Grid ESO — System Frequency (1-minute)
license: National Grid ESO Open Data License
contamination_reasoning: Live operational data; future frequency values are strictly future events.
difficulty_note: Highly chaotic, non-linear series that stays within a tight bound (49.5-50.5Hz) but exhibits micro-fluctuations. Trivial for naive baselines to get low MSE, but very hard to predict directional shifts.
adapter_notes: Requires BMRS API key. Data is available at 1-minute or 15-second resolution depending on the endpoint.
verify: Confirm the exact endpoint for 1-minute resolution historical and live data on the BMRS portal.

name: FDA Recalls, Market Withdrawals, & Safety Alerts
contamination_reasoning: Event-driven RSS feed. Future recalls cannot be pretrained on.
difficulty_note: Count/discrete series of recall events. Highly sparse, bursty, and driven by external shocks (contamination discoveries). Impossible for seasonal-naive models.
adapter_notes: Parse RSS feed. Can be aggregated to daily/weekly counts, but raw timestamp event arrival is the most challenging target.
verify: Check historical depth of the RSS feed; may need to scrape FDA archives for older events.

name: Alberta Health Services — Emergency Department Wait Times
contamination_reasoning: Live operational metric scraped from public dashboard. Obscure enough to avoid pretraining.
difficulty_note: Multi-seasonal (daily, weekly) but heavily influenced by sudden regime shifts (e.g., flu surges, trauma events). Tests models on bounded, non-stationary operational data.
adapter_notes: Requires HTML scraping as there is no official API. Updates roughly every 1-5 minutes depending on the hospital.
verify: Verify scraping legality and structure of the AHS wait times page.

name: NOAA NWS Tsunami Warning Messages (PTWC/NTWC)
contamination_reasoning: Event-driven alerts. Future seismic events cannot be in pretraining.
difficulty_note: Extreme zero-inflation. The series is flat zero for months, then spikes with event arrivals. Tests a model's ability to predict rare, discrete events.
adapter_notes: Parse XML/RSS feeds from the Pacific and National Tsunami Warning Centers.
verify: Verify the exact XML feed URL and historical availability.

name: Kickstarter — Project Launches & Funding Milestones
contamination_reasoning: Live event stream of new project launches. Future launches are unknown.
difficulty_note: Count of discrete events (launches) over time. Exhibits daily/weekly seasonality but with heavy-tailed bursts driven by external trends.
adapter_notes: Unofficial JSON endpoint. Paginate using page parameter. Rate limits apply.
verify: Confirm the unofficial JSON endpoint is still active and accessible without auth.

name: Environment Agency UK Rainfall Gauges
estimated_series_count: 1 (per station, scalable to panel)
contamination_reasoning: Live sensor data from a specific regional network. Unlikely to be in pretraining corpora, and future data is guaranteed unseen.
difficulty_note: Rainfall is highly non-Gaussian, zero-inflated, and spatially correlated. Models must handle intermittent bursts and dry spells.
adapter_notes: REST API with JSON response. Filter by `parameter=rainfall` and station ID. Use `_sorted` for chronological order.
verify: Check if the specific station ID (5229) is active and provides 15-min readings.

name: WSDOT Ferries Vessel Locations
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregated vessel positions)
contamination_reasoning: Real-time vessel tracking data from a regional ferry system. Highly specific and unlikely to be pretrain material.
difficulty_note: Vessel arrivals and departures are event-driven, with delays due to weather and tides. Requires modeling arrival times and queue lengths.
adapter_notes: Requires a free WSDOT API access code. Returns JSON array of vessel statuses. Poll every 1-5 minutes for event detection.
verify: Confirm the API access code registration process and data fields.

name: National Grid ESO System Frequency
contamination_reasoning: High-frequency (1-second/1-minute) grid frequency data. Niche operational data, unlikely to be in standard TSFM corpora. Live data is available via API or delayed bulk download.
difficulty_note: Grid frequency is a mean-reverting but volatile series sensitive to sudden generation or load trips. Requires modeling fast dynamics and regime shifts.
adapter_notes: Data available as zipped CSVs by month. A live API endpoint may exist but needs verification. If not, use a delayed bulk download and simulate live tasks with a rolling window.
verify: Check if a real-time API endpoint exists for system frequency, or if only historical bulk downloads are available.

name: ENTSO-E Transparency Platform — generation unit unavailability
license: ENTSO-E Transparency Platform — free API key for research
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate unavailability MW) or N (by unit/country)
contamination_reasoning: ENTSO-E Transparency Platform data is European grid operational data, less commonly used in ML benchmarks than US ISO data (CAISO, NYISO, PJM). The unavailability feed (planned/unplanned outages) is a niche operational dataset unlikely in TSFM pretraining. Post-cutoff data is contamination-free.
difficulty_note: Outage events have irregular start/end times, varying duration and MW impact. Unplanned outages create step changes in available capacity. The series mixes scheduled maintenance (seasonal pattern) with forced outages (exogenous, weather/fault-driven), creating a regime-switching signal that separates models.
adapter_notes: Register at transparency.entsoe.eu for free API key. Document type A80 = generation/unavailability. Use processType A16 for realised. Specify in_Domain (control area code, e.g., 10YCZ-CEPS-----N for Czechia). Returns XML/JSON with time-series of unavailable capacity. Rate limit: not strictly enforced but be reasonable.
verify: Confirm the API key registration process works and that documentType=A80 returns unavailability data. Check that multiple control areas can be queried for a panel.

name: OpenSea NFT collection sale events
license: OpenSea API — free API key required
estimated_series_count: 5-10 (by collection)
contamination_reasoning: NFT sale events are blockchain transaction records, not a standard time-series dataset. While blockchain data is public, the *event arrival rate* for specific collections is niche and unlikely in TSFM pretraining. The API requires a key, adding friction. Post-cutoff events are contamination-free.
difficulty_note: Sale event arrival is extremely bursty: collection launches, celebrity endorsements, and market sentiment create viral spikes followed by long tails. The series is zero-inflated, heavy-tailed, and regime-switching (bull/bear crypto market phases). Seasonal-naive completely fails.
adapter_notes: Register at docs.opensea.io for API key. Query by collection_slug. Returns events with timestamp, price (ETH/USD), buyer/seller addresses. Paginate with next cursor. Rate limit: 4 req/sec with key. Aggregate to event counts or volume per time bucket.
verify: Confirm the v2 API endpoint and key registration process. Check that multiple active collections provide sufficient event volume. Verify that historical events are accessible for backfill.

name: Nightscout open CGM glucose monitoring (community-hosted)
license: AGPL-3.0 (Nightscout) — data is patient-shared, research use
estimated_series_count: 5-10 (multi-patient panel from public instances)
estimated_length: P1-5Y per patient + live (5-min native cadence)
contamination_reasoning: Nightscout is an open-source DIY diabetes monitoring platform. Public instances are operated by individual patients sharing their own data. This data is extremely obscure, unstructured, and has never been packaged as a benchmark. It is essentially impossible that any TSFM has seen it during pretraining.
gap_filled: healthcare × few-min (currently 0 sources, target 3); also healthcare × half-hour if downsampled
difficulty_note: Continuous glucose monitor (CGM) data has strong autocorrelation but with meal-driven spikes, exercise-driven dips, sleep/wake regime shifts, and sensor noise. The signal is non-stationary within-patient (insulin sensitivity varies day-to-day) and across-patients (type 1 vs type 2 diabetes, different therapy regimens). This is a genuinely hard physiological forecasting problem.
adapter_notes: No central registry of public Nightscout sites. Find public instances via community directories (e.g., nightscout.github.io, cgm-remote-monitor). Each site exposes /api/v1/entries.json returning SGV (sensor glucose value), date, direction. Poll every 5-15 min. Must identify stable, long-running public instances. Data quality varies — some sites go offline.
verify: Identify 5-10 stable public Nightscout instances with at least 1 year of continuous data. Confirm the API returns consistent JSON format. Check data continuity and missing-value rate. Consider whether patient privacy / IRB review is needed even for publicly shared data.

name: TfL road network disruptions (all roads)
estimated_series_count: 1 (aggregate disruption count) or N (by road category)
contamination_reasoning: Road disruption events are real-time operational data, not a packaged dataset. The TfL Unified API is well-known but this specific endpoint (disruption events with start/end timestamps) is not a standard TS benchmark. Future disruptions cannot have been pretrained on.
difficulty_note: Disruption events have irregular start/end times, varying severity, and correlate with weather, incidents, and scheduled works. Count of active disruptions at any moment is a non-stationary point process with diurnal and weekly patterns plus exogenous event-driven spikes.
adapter_notes: No auth required. Returns JSON array of current disruptions with startDateTime, endDateTime, severity, category, road name. Poll every few minutes for live stream. Note: only returns *current* disruptions — historical archive may need separate scraping or the TfL data portal.
verify: Confirm the endpoint returns disruption events with timestamps. Check whether historical disruption data is available via TfL's open data portal for backfill.
